q: The field lines around a bar magnet form:
sol: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed curves that emerge from the North pole and enter the South pole.

q: The SI unit of magnetic field strength is:
sol: Tesla (T) is the SI unit named after Nikola Tesla used to measure magnetic induction.

q: Who discovered that a current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field?
sol: Oersted discovered the magnetic effect of electric current in 1820.

q: Magnetic field lines emerge from the ________ pole of a magnet.
sol: Outside a magnet, the direction of magnetic field lines is from the North pole to the South pole.

q: The magnetic field inside a long straight solenoid carrying current is:
sol: The field lines inside a solenoid are parallel straight lines, indicating a uniform magnetic field.

q: A compass needle is a small:
sol: A compass needle is essentially a small bar magnet whose ends point toward North and South.

q: What happens to the magnetic field strength of a solenoid if the number of turns increases?
sol: The magnetic field is directly proportional to the number of turns in the coil.

q: The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is maximum when the angle is:
sol: According to F = BIl sinθ, the force is maximum when the conductor is perpendicular to the field (90°).

q: Which rule is used to find the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field?
sol: Fleming's Left-hand rule relates the directions of force, magnetic field, and current.

q: A soft iron core placed inside a solenoid forms an:
sol: A current-carrying coil with a soft iron core is called an electromagnet.

q: The pattern of magnetic field lines around a straight current-carrying conductor is:
sol: The magnetic field lines are concentric circles with the wire at the center.

q: In Fleming's Left-hand rule, the forefinger represents:
sol: The Forefinger points in the direction of the Magnetic Field.

q: In Fleming's Left-hand rule, the middle finger represents:
sol: The Middle finger points in the direction of the Current.

q: The Right-hand thumb rule is used to determine the direction of:
sol: It determines the direction of the magnetic field produced by a straight current-carrying wire.

q: Magnetic field lines ________ each other.
sol: If they intersected, the compass would point in two directions at once, which is impossible.

q: The force exerted on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field was first researched by:
sol: Andre Marie Ampere suggested that if current produces a field, the magnet should exert a force on the current.

q: If the current in a straight wire flows vertically upwards, the magnetic field (viewed from top) is:
sol: Using the right-hand thumb rule, the fingers wrap in an anticlockwise direction.

q: Which of the following materials is used to make a permanent magnet?
sol: Steel retains its magnetism even after the external field is removed, unlike soft iron.

q: At the time of a short circuit, the current in the circuit:
sol: A short circuit occurs when live and neutral wires touch, leading to very low resistance and high current.

q: The fuse in a domestic circuit is connected in:
sol: A fuse must be in series with the live wire to break the circuit during excess current.

q: The color of the insulation for the Earth wire is usually:
sol: Green (or yellow-green) is the standard color for the earth safety wire.

q: Which wire in a domestic circuit is at a potential of 220 V?
sol: The live wire (red insulation) carries the high potential in India.

q: The potential difference between live wire and neutral wire in India is:
sol: Standard domestic power supply in India is 220 V.

q: Overloading in a circuit can occur due to:
sol: Overloading occurs when the total current exceeds the capacity of the wires.

q: Magnetic field strength is measured by the ________ of field lines.
sol: The degree of closeness of the field lines indicates the relative strength of the magnetic field.

q: Inside a magnet, the direction of field lines is:
sol: Field lines are continuous; they go N to S outside and S to N inside.

q: An alpha particle (positive) is projected towards North and deflected West by a magnetic field. The direction of the field is:
sol: Using Fleming's Left-hand rule: Current (North), Force (West), resulting field is Upward.

q: The magnetic field produced by a circular loop is maximum at the:
sol: At the center, all segments of the loop contribute to a field in the same direction.

q: A commercial electric motor does NOT use:
sol: Commercial motors use electromagnets instead of permanent magnets for greater power.

q: Which of the following properties of a proton can change while it moves freely in a magnetic field?
sol: Velocity changes because the direction of motion changes due to the magnetic force.

q: A device that reverses the direction of flow of current through a circuit is called a:
sol: In a DC motor, the split ring acts as a commutator.

q: The phenomenon of electromagnetic induction was discovered by:
sol: Faraday discovered that a changing magnetic field induces an electric current.

q: The function of Earth wire in an appliance is to:
sol: It provides a low-resistance path for leakage current to flow into the ground.

q: Magnetic field is a ________ quantity.
sol: It has both magnitude and direction.

q: The magnetic field lines of a solenoid resemble those of a:
sol: A solenoid acts exactly like a bar magnet with N and S poles.

q: Earth's magnetic field points approximately from:
sol: The Earth's magnetic North is near the geographic South pole.

q: What is the frequency of AC supply in India?
sol: The alternating current in India changes direction 100 times per second (50 cycles).

q: A solenoid carrying current is hung freely. It will rest in:
sol: It behaves like a bar magnet and aligns with Earth's magnetic field.

q: The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is zero if the angle is:
sol: If the conductor is parallel to the field lines, no magnetic force is exerted.

q: Which part of the motor makes the contact with the external circuit battery?
sol: Carbon brushes maintain sliding contact with the rotating split rings.

q: A Galvanometer is used to:
sol: It is a sensitive instrument that detects even small currents and their direction.

q: The live wire is also known as the:
sol: The live wire is technically the phase wire in a power system.

q: The North pole of a magnet is attracted towards:
sol: Like poles repel, and opposite poles attract.

q: The magnetic effect of current is used in:
sol: An electric bell uses an electromagnet to strike the gong.

q: If a magnet is broken into two pieces:
o: Each piece has N and S poles
sol: Magnetic monopoles do not exist; every piece will have two poles.

q: A current that changes direction after equal intervals of time is:
sol: This is the definition of AC (Alternating Current).

q: The main advantage of AC over DC is:
o: AC can be transmitted over long distances with less loss
sol: AC can be stepped up or down easily using transformers for efficient transmission.

q: The process of inducing current by moving a magnet is:
sol: Relative motion between a conductor and a field induces a current.

q: In domestic wiring, the neutral wire usually has insulation of:
sol: Black is the standard color for the neutral wire insulation.

q: If the strength of the current in a wire increases, the magnetic field:
sol: Field strength B is directly proportional to current I.

q: A rectangular coil of copper wires is rotated in a magnetic field. The direction of induced current changes once in each:
sol: In an AC generator, the direction of current reverses every half turn.

q: Which rule helps in finding the direction of induced current?
sol: Fleming's Right-hand rule is specifically for electromagnetic induction.

q: In Fleming's Right-hand rule, the thumb indicates:
sol: The thumb represents the movement of the conductor.

q: The strength of a magnetic field is ________ at the poles of a bar magnet.
sol: Field lines are most crowded at the poles, indicating the strongest field.

q: When current is passed through a circular loop, the field lines at the center are:
sol: At the very center of a loop, the field lines appear as straight lines perpendicular to the loop plane.

q: What creates a magnetic field?
sol: A magnetic field is always produced by moving electric charges (current).

q: The device used to produce electric current is called a:
sol: A generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.

q: Short circuiting occurs when:
sol: Direct contact between live and neutral wires leads to a short circuit.

q: An electromagnet is a:
sol: It remains a magnet only as long as current flows through the coil.

q: Magnetic field lines are ________ in a uniform magnetic field.
sol: Parallel straight lines represent a uniform field, such as inside a solenoid.

q: To get a strong magnetic field in a solenoid, we should use a core of:
sol: Soft iron is easily magnetized and significantly boosts the field strength.

q: Which of the following determines the polarity of a solenoid?
sol: Clock-face rule or Right-hand rule determines which end is North or South based on current direction.

q: The force on a moving charge in a magnetic field is zero if the charge is moving:
sol: Magnetic force F = qvB sinθ is zero when θ = 0° or 180°.

q: In an electric motor, the energy transformation is:
sol: A motor uses electricity to produce rotation (mechanical energy).

q: The 'split rings' in a DC motor are used to:
sol: They act as a commutator to ensure the coil rotates in the same direction.

q: Which instrument uses electromagnetic induction to work?
sol: Microphones and Generators work on the principle of EMI.

q: Magnetic field strength is ________ proportional to the distance from a straight wire.
sol: As the distance from the wire increases, the magnetic field strength decreases.

q: The compass needle points towards the:
sol: By convention, the 'North' end of a compass points to Geographic North.

q: Maxwell’s Corkscrew rule is another name for:
sol: Both rules describe the circular magnetic field around a straight conductor.

q: A positively charged particle moving West is deflected North by a magnetic field. The field direction is:
sol: Using Fleming's Left-hand rule, the field must be directed downwards.

q: Alternating current (AC) is preferred for long-distance transmission because of:
sol: High-voltage AC transmission significantly reduces heat loss in wires.

q: The magnetic field lines at the poles of a solenoid are:
sol: Just like a bar magnet, lines emerge from N and enter S.

q: What happens to a current-carrying conductor when placed in a magnetic field?
sol: This is the principle behind the working of an electric motor.

q: In India, the frequency of 50 Hz means the current reverses direction every:
sol: One cycle has two reversals. 50 cycles per second means 100 reversals.

q: The metal casing of high-power electrical appliances is earthed to:
sol: Earthing ensures that any leakage to the metal body flows to the ground instead of the user.

q: Which of the following is NOT attracted by a magnet?
sol: Aluminium is a non-ferromagnetic material.

q: A solenoid is a ________ coil of many circular turns of insulated copper wire.
sol: Its length is much larger than its diameter.

q: A permanent magnet's magnetism can be destroyed by:
sol: These methods disrupt the alignment of magnetic domains.

q: Which scientist gave the 'Left-hand rule'?
sol: John Ambrose Fleming devised the rules for motor and generator directions.

q: The magnetic field lines of a straight wire are ________ to the wire.
o: In planes perpendicular to the wire
sol: The lines form circles in planes that are perpendicular to the length of the wire.

q: When a magnet is moved towards a coil, the induced current depends on:
sol: All these factors influence the rate of change of magnetic flux.

q: In a house, the 'mains' supply comes through:
sol: Domestic supply involves Live, Neutral, and Earth wires.

q: The force between two parallel wires carrying current is due to:
sol: The magnetic fields of the two currents interact to produce a force.

q: What is 'Armature' in an electric motor?
sol: The soft iron core on which the coil is wound is called the armature.

q: A current-carrying solenoid behaves like a:
sol: It creates a magnetic field pattern identical to a bar magnet.

q: In Fleming's Right-hand rule, the middle finger shows:
sol: Forefinger-Field, Thumb-Motion, Middle-Induced Current.

q: The magnetic field produced by a solenoid is ________ inside it.
sol: The field lines are parallel straight lines inside.

q: Galvanometer deflection to the right indicates current in one direction; deflection to the left indicates:
sol: Deflection direction depends on the direction of current flow.

q: Electricity is generated in power plants using:
sol: Large generators convert mechanical energy (from steam/water) into electricity.

q: The Earth's magnetic poles are ________ to its geographic poles.
sol: There is an offset of about 11-15 degrees between geographic and magnetic poles.

q: Safety fuse is based on the ________ effect of current.
sol: The fuse melts due to heat produced by high current.

q: Standard color for Live wire insulation in modern wiring is:
sol: Red (old) or Brown (new) is used for live wires.

q: When current in a circular loop is clockwise (viewed from one side), that side acts as:
sol: According to the Clock-face rule, clockwise current indicates a South pole.

q: The core of an electromagnet should be made of soft iron because it:
o: Loses magnetism quickly when current stops
sol: Soft iron has low retentivity, making it perfect for temporary magnets.

q: One Tesla is equal to:
sol: Tesla is also defined as magnetic flux per unit area (Wb/m²).

q: If we reverse the direction of current in a wire, the magnetic field lines will:
sol: The field direction depends entirely on the current direction.

q: Domestic electrical appliances are not connected in series because:
o: They would all have to be 'ON' at once
sol: In series, if one appliance is off, the circuit is broken for all.

q: A compass needle is deflected when brought near a current-carrying wire because of:
sol: The magnetic field of the wire exerts a force on the magnetic needle.

q: The force on a current-carrying conductor is maximum when it is ________ to the magnetic field.
sol: Maximum force occurs at 90 degrees.

q: Fleming's Left-hand rule is used in the construction of:
sol: Motors work on the principle of force on current in a magnetic field.
